speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Eli Sommer, tape number three. Mr. Sommer, what happened after your family was taken? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in town to get [? those-- ?] my [? small ?] [? at the ?] [? time. ?] And now, actually, they brought people from outside, because they liquidated a lot of small towns. And they felt our town was very good size. They brought us over. They brought people to town. And same thing. They had to go to work. They established ghetto A, ghetto B. You know, ghetto B was supposed to be for people who went to work, and ghetto A for-- for people who didn't go to work. But it was blurred actually. Not too much. [SIGHS] But it was divided, so that if you had to go or had to [INAUDIBLE] permission to go from ghetto A to B. But I don't know that it meant that much. It was not too hard to go from one ghetto to the other one. And actually, it was quiet. Quiet. There was [? their ?] normal thing. Before I called outside and now, in papers, the report. And they were usually killed in ghetto, and you had to bury them. And people were going to work. And that's still, there was the other one. The-- the-- a year later, it was in September, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1943, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me, when-- when was the second action, when your relatives were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the second action was in September 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1943, they-- it wasn't actually the last action, actually. And it was the same thing. They divided people, people who were still going to work. It was separated, and people who were not going-- who-- who didn't have, or they worked in places that was not so much acknowledged by them as very necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow, they invited part people, or a part of the transport went to Krakow-Plaszow. This we knew. The other one was going somewhere else, nobody knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I remember I-- I had a very good friend, and he was going to-- to right in front of the gate from ghetto. And he was asking me, let's go together. And I said, listen, I don't-- I didn't take a-- I didn't have a blanket with me or anything to take with me. And so I said, I'm going to go home, and I'm going to come back, and we go together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the way, a friend of mine told me that her father is staying. So I said to her, why-- what do you mean he's staying? He said-- he said, yes, they are. A certain amount of people are going to stay here. So-- so I got very interested in it, and I finally found where the people are staying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a one barrack that they used to keep the people separately. And somehow, I got into-- into this barrack, and I stayed. And all the people, one part of the people who went to Plaszow, to Krakow-Plaszow. And one part was east. And from later on, I found out it was [INAUDIBLE]. And it was the same place that my family went. And nobody-- nobody survived from this transport. That was the transport that I was supposed to go with my friend, going east. And I-- I-- the ghetto was liquidated. And they left a few hundred, I believe 300. I don't exactly remember already how many, but there was a few hundred people. And we were staying, and we're liquidators. We were cleaning up the apartments. And-- and there were all kind of places that they were repairing things and washing, big laundry, and-- and preparing for-- for transport. And I was one of the people, 12 people, loading the trains where the merchandise were going out to the train and-- and-- or cleaning up or taking out the furniture from that apartments and later loading to the trains. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they sending the things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything to Germany. Everything was going to Germany. Clean things, and the apartment. You clean up the apartment, naturally. Then-- then once the-- the apartments were cleaned, [? when ?] the sections would [? ghetto, ?] they-- they made it [? by ?] smaller ghettos. And that's how it-- that's how it was. And this period was very bad. I remember this-- this period left a very bad time, a very bad. You know, you saw a lot of tragic things. A lot of people were, when they made the ghetto smaller, a lot of people were hiding in the apartments, in the houses. And they were caught. And they were bringing those people through, I remember, like through Polish section, because the sections were-- in Polish sections. And I remember people were found while-- I remember 11 or 12 people, they took it by the rope and like-- like-- like-- and they were leading them by the neck, one bound to another one, through the streets. I guess that's to show people within Poland, the Polish people, to-- to be careful and not to-- not to-- to take any people to hide. To-- to-- to-- and they brought them over. I mean, that was a daily appearance, daily things. It's not something once happened. It happened almost every day. And we have on this square, they built as like a pyre. I don't know, a pyre, you know, when they put one layer of wood, then one layer, or after they brought them, and they shot them, one layer of, you know, of people, of-- of dead people. And the fire was going on as long as I remember. For-- it was sitting there from, I don't know, October, I believe. I don't remember. I think October, till we left the-- the ghetto. There was always fire going on. It would smell the-- the flesh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding the ghetto while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto, [INAUDIBLE] Polish police on the outside. And there was-- and there's supposed to be a Jewish police inside. But outside is Polish police. And naturally, it was so we had a commander, a guy who was actually a peasant. And he was in charge of the-- of the-- of the German police, sort of. And gestapo was coming every day, almost every day. And I don't know what kind of business they had. And usually, they shot a few people. Or-- or-- or they look for somebody, or whatever. Or if they needed something, or they [? leave ?] any directives, they-- they were coming. And-- and that's what it-- everybody had to be on the toe all the time, because we never knew when it's going-- somebody is going to come. And.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was the head of the-- of the-- the Jewish head of the ghetto? Do you know the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was [? Falkman, ?] at that time, [? Falkman. ?] And another one was, how do you call? I forgot the name already. Anyway, but they had to do what they were told. I mean, there's nothing, nothing they could do. I mean, they-- they put up, I don't  know, if they wanted to be or not, whatever they, or what was the reason why they were, but they had to do whatever they were told. Otherwise, the families would be jeopardy. And they-- they had somebody to represent the Jewish people. And-- and so the Germans, they would come and ask them for-- for certain things. [INAUDIBLE] was the other one. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of running away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I thought that many things, many times, actually, to run away. But I never somehow did. First, that was even before ghetto, we had a woman, a younger woman, who was working for my sister yet. And she offered-- [INAUDIBLE] she was about 20. And she-- I think she was married, and her husband was-- went to war in the army. And she lived outside of town. Or-- and she asked me to come. And she told me that you can-- she was going to find a place, and she's going to hide me somewhere. And-- but I was afraid. I-- first of all, at that time, we didn't know. Later, and we find out what has happened, I mean, they-- I would know what was going to happen, naturally, maybe I would act differently. And then, while in ghetto, almost-- almost everybody had some kind of papers, you know, false papers. And I had the papers, false papers. But when you keep the papers, you're afraid to-- to keep on yourself, naturally. So I-- I put them in the-- in the-- in the-- how do you call, the-- in the attic. I thought that I-- I-- I had-- that they were hidden right. And somebody found it. It so happened, it was a Jewish guy. And he told me about this. And he said, see, I'm very careless, you know. [INAUDIBLE] anybody here in Germany could find it. But what can you-- it was no-- no safe place to find. So-- so-- I-- I kept it, just in case, those papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody was always making something. You know. They were false, but they-- they look identical. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you could use it in an emergency. Naturally, I don't know if-- if they would catch you. You could just [INAUDIBLE]. You could-- you could-- who was I? I mean, the different name and the Polish name, or something. But you had, just in case. And I never used it. As a matter of fact, I remember before I went to Plaszow. And when I was in [INAUDIBLE], they call it, I and other friends of mine were planning to-- to run as-- to Polish section. And he had somebody where we should go. And I took the papers. And somehow, and we prepared ourself. We had some-- I had some in my family-- from family, which we were hid. You know, we hid in-- somehow in different places. And there we-- we put-- somebody had a very-- some very honest people. And they were-- and they were supposed to keep the money in case we were going somewhere, at whenever would be, a Polish section or in camp. We knew we didn't know how it's going to work out. And so we put some money. And-- and-- and some we had from my mother, the diamonds ring, and all kinds of heirlooms, whatever it was, and some money in gold. And-- but we were, somehow, the last day, we were, as I said before, we're-- we're loading. And we had two or three carriages that we-- that we were taking to the-- to the train. And there's my friend, was one way, and I was going the other way. But it was 12 o'clock. And the last day, we found ourself in ghetto. I guess our heart was not to-- to-- to be. You know, and then we both went to-- to Plaszow. And we-- we came there. I believe it was February the 8th, 1940, '45. No, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave the ghetto? what was date that you left the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto? No, that was February 8, I believe. That's what I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they tell you that you were going to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the loaded us on the-- on the-- on the car. It was a freight car, you know, like a panel cars, panel cars. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nothing. I mean, whatever we have on. We didn't take nothing. Whatever-- we didn't have anything. That's why I had a very hard time in Plaszow. I didn't have money. And Plaszow was also-- people-- you know, it was a concentration camp. But this camp was located in Krakow. It was a-- a-- a was Plaszow. And that's where they located, they make, a concentration camp, on the-- on the hill. And a lot of people, most people, were from Praszow-- from Krakow. So they had connections with Polish people in Krakow. So they could help themselves. The same as we were doing in ghetto, they could-- they took to the camp. So they were helping themselves somehow. They were trade-- trading, and they were selling, when there-- and they allowed-- some people were going, not too many, but some people were going to work in-- in-- in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whoever who-- had some money or something to-- to trade could help [INAUDIBLE], some who had additional food. But we came, and I didn't have nothing. And I didn't know anybody. And-- and I got the worst place to work. Well, describe what happened when you arrived at Plaszow. What was the first thing that happened when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they assigned us to-- to certain barracks. And then they assigned us to certain work in-- to go to. And the barracks were the worst, because whoever didn't have money, and they were [? last ?] one. Slowly, whoever had some money could help himself. And we didn't have money. I didn't have money. I mean, I didn't have nothing to trade, so I was assigned to worst work. And then whenever they had some kind of-- somehow, they were always sending people away, to new camps or bad camps or better places. But you never knew. You know, when you-- when you were at a place, you knew the place where you were, and you knew what you had to expect. If you came to Plaszow, it was very bad, but you knew what it was. Now, you didn't know where they were sending you, and if-- if this was a people-- a-- a place to-- to kill or to live. So whenever they wanted to send us out, somehow they went-- the people couldn't help themself. And I was on the list, because I had the worst place. I couldn't buy myself out. Now, I had, as I mentioned before, I put away some jewelry and money, whatever I had, and put in with somebody who I didn't know, but through my friend. And he was supposed to be a very honest man. And-- but it took time till you get. You come to Plaszow, and it was-- everything was very dangerous. I mean, you came to a new place. You had to know what to meet. You had to get some contact. And-- and some people who were more closely with this process, they were first to get their things. They were first. So by the time I was supposed to get, they were saying it's already in Krakow, and I-- I'd be able to get, a few-- some money, then it would be better. But then they shipped me out, and I didn't want to stay in Plaszow anymore. I didn't want to stay one more. I could stay, because I-- friends of mine, who got already their something, wanted to give me money and all. But I said, no, I want to get out of [INAUDIBLE] here. I had a very bad time there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was putting on the pick-- picks, on the [? styles ?] on the picks. And all day, that was the work I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with friends there? Did you know anyone there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I didn't, unless the people who I came from. I don't remember who-- who was there. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the guards in the barracks there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks were people who, you know, it was always assigned somebody, usually people who had a little bit protection, a little bit, knew somebody, you know. And they were-- they were people who were staying. And they tried to have an easy job, not to go out and not to work hard. So they were in charge of it. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what nationality were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they're all Jews. In Plaszow were all Jews, all Jews. They were Jewish, came from Krakow. Because that was-- Krakow was established when they liquidated. If you saw the that was-- that was exactly what they showed when liquidation of ghetto Krakow, and they went to-- to-- to Plaszow. That's-- they were showed how they liquidated the ghetto, and it was pretty realistic, I have to say. Pretty realistic. And that was the Plaszow. That's where I was. And I remember I decided to leave after they took me, and they decided that they are going to ship me. They are going to send me somewhere, to the new camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there selections in Plaszow? Were there selections?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selection? Not [? when ?] I came. [INAUDIBLE] get, get. When there was-- in-- in he was the commandant. And-- [INAUDIBLE] but there were always selections. They were always killing. And that was every day, but I was not that privy to it. I did-- I was not the one who remembered seeing it. But they-- they called it-- it was a place that they used to take people you shoot. I mean, it was a little bit outside of-- of the camp. And I mean, these things were coming, whoever. I mean, that was pretty realistic, that they showed in

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there appels there also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, every day. Oh, the appel-- appels were always. Before you go to work, you had to go appel. They're counting every day. They were counting in the morning. And you went after work. They counting, and everybody is there. That was always, always going on. That was part of the German-- German tradition, I guess, from the-- they-- they run like a-- like a camp, a real camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when I was in camp, I was, first of all, as I said, I-- I mentioned that I have some money outside of camp. And I had some-- some things, but I couldn't get it yet. So I have a friend who got already money. And there was a couple, actually. And she came over. [? She ?] said, listen, I know you don't want to wait, and because I decided to go, if you want to, I can give you meanwhile whatever you need. So you don't have to go maybe, because always could [INAUDIBLE]. I said no, I want to go. I had a bad time. So she said-- it was very nice of her. She gave me her ring. And she said, in case you don't know where you go, in case something happen, you have to get it and take it with me. And-- and I took this-- this ring with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to ask you, who would you give the money to so that you would get a better place?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were always people in between anywhere, in camp or concentration camp or in ghetto. For money, you could always buy yourself out somehow. There were always people who knew somebody, and who, I mean, instead of you, they took somebody else. And meanwhile, he got something. I mean, I mean, that's what's going on here, too. With money, you can buy yourself out, no matter what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- so she gave me the ring. And we-- they shipped us by train. That was the first transport from Plaszow to Germany. It was the beginning of May. I think it was-- I know the 8 is somehow in my head. I don't know exactly the date, but that was the beginning of May. And they shipped us in-- in wagons. And they-- freight wagon. How many people were?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, it was not too bad, actually. I don't remember that it would be so crowded. It was not that crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this, Mr. Sommer?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1944, May 1944. And they shipped us to Gross-Rosen. And that was a concentration camp. And it was-- it didn't have a good name. It was pretty rough concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mean that there were pretty-- the condition were not so good. And they were-- they were killing. And they were-- they had-- and they had, you know, their burning people-- the-- the stoves, they were in there, whatever, how they called it. And we were in quarantine for two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I guess we were not assigned to stay in Gross-Rosen. We were not assigned to stay in Gross-Rosen. They usually took people, like a staging, like a camp. And then they distributed to different camps. Unless you were staying there for a steady, then they are-- you know, first day, you came-- when you came in, and the-- and the entrance was [SPEAKING GERMAN]. The-- you-- the work makes you free. And the-- the-- the music was on-- at-- at the gate. And we were so surprised. I never heard so music.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of music?

speaker: interviewee
text: Music, some kind-- you know, like a band. And the guy-- and the guys who were-- usually, they-- I think they were Volksdeutsches or some Ukrainians or [INAUDIBLE], from Latvia, [INAUDIBLE]. The people were guarding us. So they said, this was a-- this was Volksdeutsche. You know, a Volksdeutsche was a man who was of German descent. And they said, oh, you have it German. No, that's-- that's a bad camp when music is  staying at the gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right there, they took us to [INAUDIBLE] to clean up whatever we had, in case we have some fleas or whatever. And they-- they shaved our heads and shaved our everywhere. And we're naked. We came out naked on the side of the-- of the barracks, where [INAUDIBLE] was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they started to look what we have, what else. And I had this ring. And I didn't know what to do with it. So being I was naked, completely naked, and they were looking in the ears and everywhere. So I-- I-- I had teeth at the time, good teeth. And I-- I somehow broke the ring-- and-- and swallowed the diamond. And-- and then they gave us the striped pants like a pajamas. That's what we wore, or had. And then they assigned us to barracks. And there were about four in one, and one small, very small, bed-- bed like a [INAUDIBLE], they call them. And I went there for two weeks, and I couldn't find the diamond, because the latrine was right next to the-- next to the electric-- electric wire-- electric, how do you call, electric gate, an [INAUDIBLE] gates. Anyway. And at the other side were the Germans. Whether-- whenever you are started to looking, when you went to the bathroom, it said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] and with a gun. And I never found it. It was this story with the ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work at Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't work in Gross-Rosen. Gross-Rosen was strictly quarantine. And we had to get up in the morning. And that was in the mountains. And at that time, it was very cold. That's in Silesia. And it was cold. And they-- they got us up at-- at 4 o'clock, 4:30 in the morning. We had to stay till 6 o'clock at night, 7:00-- till it was dark. We stayed. And everybody-- everybody was next to each other, like-- like you see cows out in winter time. And one was next to each other. That's how we [? survived, ?] all day.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll-- we'll continue on the next tape.
